relevant_files.role: Core regex-based pattern engine that scans line-by-line; needs AST alternative
Orchestrates security scanning for diffs and files; interfaces with PatternEngine
Defines Finding, PatternDefinition, Severity, Confidence data structures
Determines when to trigger security scans based on PR context
LLM-based validator that reduces false positives via batched AI calls
14 regex patterns for SQL injection, secrets, path traversal, XSS, crypto, etc.
Integration tests documenting multi-line vulnerability limitation
Workspace dependencies; currently uses regex=1, no tree-sitter
Conventional commits, GPG signing, DCO sign-off required

conventions.commits: Conventional Commits (feat:, fix:, chore:, etc.), GPG signed with DCO sign-off required, no co-authors
conventions.testing: Unit tests in modules, integration tests in crates/aptu-core/tests/, AAA pattern, fixture-based testing for security patterns
conventions.linting: cargo clippy, cargo fmt, actionlint for workflows, workspace-level lint configs
conventions.error_handling: anyhow::Result for error propagation, Context trait for error messages, no thiserror in security module

patterns: Diff parsing to extract file paths and line numbers
File extension filtering in pattern matching
Integration with LLM-based validator for false positive reduction
Ignore rules via SecurityConfig for path and pattern filtering

related_issues.title: Document SecurityScanner limitation: cannot detect multi-line vulnerabilities
related_issues.relevance: Directly motivates tree-sitter evaluation; shows regex line-by-line limitation

related_issues.title: Add negative test for multi-line SQL injection detection limitation
related_issues.relevance: Merged PR documenting the limitation; test case in security_integration.rs

constraints: Binary size impact from grammar crates (tree-sitter-rust, tree-sitter-python, etc.)
Each language requires separate grammar crate dependency
Tree-sitter queries use Scheme-like syntax (learning curve for maintainers)
Data-flow/taint tracking still requires custom logic on top of AST
Must maintain backward compatibility with existing regex patterns
Diff parsing logic must be updated to support multi-line context
Performance must not regress on large diffs
test_coverage: 4 integration tests: hardcoded_secrets_detection, sql_injection_detection, safe_patterns_no_findings, multi_line_vulnerability_not_detected (documents limitation). Unit tests in patterns.rs for pattern loading and hardcoded API key detection. Benchmark suite in benches/security_scan.rs using Criterion. Test fixtures in tests/security_fixtures/vulnerable/ and tests/security_fixtures/safe/.

library_findings.notes: Core library for parsing; stable API; supports incremental parsing; query language for pattern matching

library_findings.relevant_api: LANGUAGE constant for Rust grammar; enables AST-based detection for Rust code
library_findings.notes: Rust grammar crate; maintained by tree-sitter community; covers Rust syntax comprehensively
Currently used for all 14 patterns; would be retained for simple patterns in hybrid approach

approaches.name: Hybrid Regex + Tree-Sitter (Recommended)
approaches.description: Keep regex for simple single-line patterns (hardcoded secrets, weak crypto, debug mode). Add tree-sitter-rust for multi-line patterns (SQL injection, command injection, path traversal). Create AstScanner alongside PatternEngine. Patterns.json gains optional 'ast_query' field for tree-sitter queries. SecurityScanner routes patterns to appropriate engine based on type.
pros: Minimal disruption to existing regex patterns
Leverage tree-sitter only where it adds value (multi-line detection)
Gradual migration path; can convert patterns incrementally
Reduced binary size vs full tree-sitter adoption
Easier testing: keep existing regex tests, add AST-specific tests
cons: Learning curve for tree-sitter query syntax
Need to validate AST approach matches regex detection quality

approaches.name: Full Tree-Sitter Migration
approaches.description: Replace PatternEngine entirely with AstScanner using tree-sitter. Convert all 14 regex patterns to tree-sitter queries. Support Rust, Python, JavaScript initially. Update patterns.json schema to use 'ast_query' instead of 'pattern'. Rewrite SecurityScanner to use only AstScanner. Remove regex dependency.
pros: Better handling of complex syntax (nested calls, multi-line expressions)
cons: All 14 patterns must be converted to tree-sitter queries simultaneously
Larger binary size from multiple grammar crates
Risk of detection quality regression during migration
Breaking change to pattern definition format

approaches.name: Spike: Tree-Sitter for SQL Injection Only
approaches.description: Add tree-sitter-rust as optional dependency. Create new AstScanner module with single SQL injection detector using tree-sitter queries. Keep existing PatternEngine for all other patterns. SecurityScanner tries AstScanner first for SQL patterns, falls back to regex. Evaluate performance, detection quality, and complexity before deciding on full migration.
pros: Directly addresses issue #735 limitation (multi-line SQL injection)
Minimal code changes; easy to revert if unsuccessful
Provides concrete data for migration decision
cons: Defers decision on other multi-line patterns (command injection, path traversal)
Requires later refactoring if full migration chosen
Test coverage only for SQL injection AST variant

recommendation: Approach 1 (Hybrid Regex + Tree-Sitter). Balances pragmatism with long-term vision. The spike (Approach 3) is too narrow; it solves one pattern but leaves others unsolved, requiring rework. Full migration (Approach 2) is too risky given the 14-pattern surface area and learning curve. Hybrid approach: (1) Add tree-sitter-rust as optional dependency with feature flag. (2) Create AstScanner module with tree-sitter query execution. (3) Extend PatternDefinition to include optional 'ast_query' field. (4) Update SecurityScanner to route patterns intelligently. (5) Convert 3-4 multi-line patterns (SQL injection, command injection, path traversal) to tree-sitter queries first. (6) Validate detection quality matches or exceeds regex baseline. (7) Incrementally convert remaining patterns. This preserves existing regex patterns, reduces risk, and allows staged rollout. Start with SQL injection to address issue #735 directly, then expand to other multi-line patterns based on validation results.
